title: Quantum Model for CVRPTW
abstract: This paper proposes a quantum algorithm for the capacitated vehicle routing problem with time windows (CVRPTW) based on Grover Search framework. This problem is often faced by Postal services in the context of package delivery or other time-sensitive operations. We provide an implementation on gate based quantum computer of a model inspired by classical route first, cluster second technique. The quantum paradigm allows to overcome suboptimality inherent property of this decomposition. In the current NISQ (Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum) era, the most important limitation is the number of available qubits which makes time windows and capacity constraints hard to tackle. We introduce a qubit-efficient split-inspired modeling which adds only a linear number of decision qubits to standard quantum formulations for Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP).
summary: This paper presents a new quantum algorithm for solving complex vehicle routing problems with capacity and time constraints. By using a 'split-inspired' technique, the authors significantly reduce the number of extra qubits needed compared to previous methods, making the problem more accessible to near-term quantum hardware.
main_problem: Developing an efficient quantum algorithm to solve the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (CVRPTW), which is an NP-hard optimization problem.
main_result: The authors propose a qubit-efficient quantum model that adds only a linear number of decision qubits compared to standard TSP formulations, providing an optimal approach for the NISQ era.
method: The approach uses a Grover Adaptive Search (GAS) framework based on the Dürr and Høyer algorithm, employing a 'route-first, cluster-second' strategy with a split-inspired model.
model_or_system: The model is designed for gate-based quantum computers in the NISQ era, utilizing a position-based encoding and an oracle that checks capacity and time window constraints via reversible logic.
key_observables: Total travel cost (distance) and the validity of constraints (capacity and time windows).
important_parameters: Number of customers (n), maximum vehicle capacity (Cmax), delivery time windows [ai, bi], and the distance matrix (D).
main_assumptions: The model assumes each customer is assigned a single time window and is limited by the gate complexity and qubit counts available on NISQ-era hardware.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the giant tour and the auxiliary graph used for the split procedure; Figure 2 and 3 show quantum circuit diagrams for capacity and time window constraints; Figure 4 shows qubit scaling relative to the number of customers.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CVRPTW problem, proposes a quantum algorithm based on Grover Search, details the qubit-efficient encoding and oracle construction (including capacity and time constraints), analyzes the complexity, and discusses hardware limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
